revenue_by_merchant.sql:
SELECT m.merchant_name,
       SUM(t.amount) as total_revenue,
       COUNT(*) as txn_count
FROM fact_transactions t, dim_merchant m
WHERE t.merchant_id = m.merchant_id
AND t.transaction_date > '2024-01-01'
GROUP BY m.merchant_name
ORDER BY total_revenue
LIMIT 10;

issues.title: Missing filter for transaction status
issues.problem: The query does not filter transactions by status, which means it will include all transactions, not just completed ones.

issues.title: Implicit join syntax
issues.problem: Using implicit join syntax can lead to less readable and maintainable code. It's better to use explicit JOIN syntax.
issues.fix: FROM fact_transactions t JOIN dim_merchant m ON t.merchant_id = m.merchant_id

issues.title: Lack of comments
issues.problem: The query lacks comments explaining the purpose and logic, which can be helpful for future maintenance.
issues.fix: Add comments to explain the purpose of each part of the query.

review.corrected_sql: SELECT m.merchant_name, SUM(t.amount) as total_revenue, COUNT(*) as txn_count FROM fact_transactions t JOIN dim_merchant m ON t.merchant_id = m.merchant_id WHERE t.status = 'COMPLETED' AND t.transaction_date > '2024-01-01' GROUP BY m.merchant_name ORDER BY total_revenue LIMIT 10;
review.summary: The query had a critical correctness issue by not filtering completed transactions and used an implicit join. Readability was also improved by suggesting comments.

customer_spend.sql:
SELECT c.customer_name, c.email, c.tier,
       SUM(t.amount) as lifetime_value,
       COUNT(t.transaction_id) as total_orders
FROM dim_customer c
LEFT JOIN fact_transactions t ON c.customer_id = t.customer_id
WHERE t.status = 'COMPLETED'
GROUP BY c.customer_name, c.email
HAVING SUM(t.amount) > 1000
ORDER BY lifetime_value DESC;

issues.title: Incorrect revenue calculation
issues.problem: The query is summing all transaction amounts without filtering by status 'COMPLETED'.
issues.fix: SUM(CASE WHEN t.status = 'COMPLETED' THEN t.amount ELSE 0 END) as lifetime_value

issues.title: Missing filter for total orders
issues.problem: The query is counting all transactions without filtering by status 'COMPLETED'.
issues.fix: COUNT(CASE WHEN t.status = 'COMPLETED' THEN t.transaction_id ELSE NULL END) as total_orders

issues.title: Lack of comments
issues.problem: The query lacks comments explaining the purpose and logic.
issues.fix: Add comments explaining the purpose and logic of each clause.

review.corrected_sql:
SELECT c.customer_name, c.email, c.tier,
       SUM(CASE WHEN t.status = 'COMPLETED' THEN t.amount ELSE 0 END) as lifetime_value,
       COUNT(CASE WHEN t.status = 'COMPLETED' THEN t.transaction_id ELSE NULL END) as total_orders
FROM dim_customer c
LEFT JOIN fact_transactions t ON c.customer_id = t.customer_id
GROUP BY c.customer_name, c.email
HAVING SUM(CASE WHEN t.status = 'COMPLETED' THEN t.amount ELSE 0 END) > 1000
ORDER BY lifetime_value DESC;
review.summary: The query had issues with revenue calculation and total orders count. Readability was also improved by adding comments.

daily_failure_rate.sql:
SELECT transaction_date,
       (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM fact_transactions f2
        WHERE f2.status = 'FAILED'
        AND f2.transaction_date = t.transaction_date) as failed_count,
       COUNT(*) as total_count,
       failed_count / total_count * 100 as failure_rate
FROM fact_transactions t
GROUP BY transaction_date
ORDER BY transaction_date;

issues.title: Incorrect calculation of failure rate
issues.problem: The failure rate is being calculated incorrectly because failed_count and total_count are being divided in the SELECT clause, which is not allowed.

issues.title: Correlated subquery
issues.line: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM fact_transactions f2 WHERE f2.status = 'FAILED' AND f2.transaction_date = t.transaction_date
issues.problem: The correlated subquery can be very inefficient as it is executed for each row in the outer query.
issues.fix: Use a JOIN with a pre-aggregated subquery.

issues.title: Lack of comments
issues.problem: The query lacks comments explaining the logic, which makes it harder to understand.
issues.fix: Add comments to explain the purpose of each part of the query.

issues.title: Alias for table
issues.problem: Using a single-letter alias for the table can make the query harder to read.
issues.fix: Use a more descriptive alias, e.g., 'fact_transactions t'

review.corrected_sql:
WITH daily_failures AS (SELECT transaction_date, COUNT(*) AS failed_count FROM fact_transactions WHERE status = 'FAILED' GROUP BY transaction_date)
SELECT t.transaction_date,
       COALESCE(df.failed_count, 0) AS failed_count,
       COUNT(*) AS total_count,
       COALESCE(df.failed_count, 0) * 100.0 / COUNT(*) AS failure_rate
FROM fact_transactions t
LEFT JOIN daily_failures df ON t.transaction_date = df.transaction_date
GROUP BY t.transaction_date
ORDER BY t.transaction_date;
review.summary: The query had critical correctness issues with failure rate calculation and performance issues with a correlated subquery. Readability was also improved by adding comments and using more descriptive aliases.
